item.title: RISC OS Open source developments weekly update
item.description: Here are some of the things that I spotted which happened in the RISC OS Open source world this week...
item.source:markdown: Here are some of the things that I spotted which happened in the RISC OS Open source world this week...

item.title: Gnome Working on Test Center App to Make Testing Easier
item.description: It's now possible to test experimental features on the Gnome desktop without worrying that you'll break things.
item.source:markdown: It's now possible to test experimental features on the Gnome desktop without worrying that you'll break things.
item.author: webdevel@sparkhausmedia.com (Gwen Clark)

item.title: New Vulnerability Discovered in Linux Kernel
item.description: Hiding out for nearly 15 years, the Ghostlock vulnerability allows a standard logged-in user to gain root privileges.
item.source:markdown: Hiding out for nearly 15 years, the Ghostlock vulnerability allows a standard logged-in user to gain root privileges.
item.author: webdevel@sparkhausmedia.com (Gwen Clark)

item.title: The Life and Times of Maxis, Part 2: SimWorld
item.description: This article tells part of the story of Maxis Software. Maxis came into 1994 riding high on the wings of SimCity 2000, the hit of the previous holiday season. They followed up that success with… SimHealth: The National Health Care Simulation. This may sound like satire, but it was all too real. To be clear, SimHealth was […]
item.source:markdown: This article tells part of the story of Maxis Software. Maxis came into 1994 riding high on the wings of SimCity 2000, the hit of the previous holiday season. They followed up that success with… SimHealth: The National Health Care Simulation. This may sound like satire, but it was all too real. To be clear, SimHealth was \[…\]

item.title: Mag42 – a new French-language magazine for alternative platforms
item.description: The first issue of a new French-language digital (PDF) magazine is available to buy for enthusiasts of platforms sitting a little (or even some way) outside of the mainstream – which includes RISC OS. The first issue of Mag42 covers a whopping eighteen platforms, serving as a buyers’ guide with details of their specifications, potential uses, and prices – and one of those eighteen is the Titanium, from Elesar. Containing 60 A5 pages, Mag42 is delivered as a PDF file and costs €5.90, and according to David Feugey the next…
item.source:markdown: The first issue of a new French-language digital (PDF) magazine is available to buy for enthusiasts of platforms sitting a little (or even some way) outside of the mainstream – which includes RISC OS. The first issue of Mag42 covers a whopping eighteen platforms, serving as a buyers’ guide with details of their specifications, potential uses, and prices – and one of those eighteen is the Titanium, from Elesar. Containing 60 A5 pages, Mag42 is delivered as a PDF file and costs €5.90, and according to David Feugey the next…

item.title: New Linux Flaw Lets Attackers Escape VMs
item.description: A 16-year-old vulnerability allows an attacker to escape a virtual machine, gain access to the host, and execute malicious code.
item.source:markdown: A 16-year-old vulnerability allows an attacker to escape a virtual machine, gain access to the host, and execute malicious code.
item.author: webdevel@sparkhausmedia.com (Gwen Clark)

item.title: RISC OS Open source developments weekly update
item.description: I'm going to try a new series here, where I report on the updates that have happened in Open Source over the last week, each Saturday. There are often a few things going on, and hopefully I'll be able to spot them, and report what's happening.
item.source:markdown: I'm going to try a new series here, where I report on the updates that have happened in Open Source over the last week, each Saturday. There are often a few things going on, and hopefully I'll be able to spot them, and report what's happening.

item.title: b.log 2026/07/11 - The fete, No I won't use your stupid app, The missing member, Arduino IDE looks like Zap!, Combine harvester!
item.description: The fete, No I won't use your stupid app, The missing member, Arduino IDE looks like Zap!, Combine harvester!
item.source:markdown: The fete, No I won't use your stupid app, The missing member, Arduino IDE looks like Zap!, Combine harvester!

item.title: Trubo Oberon
item.description: Oberon is Niklaus Wirth's successor to Pascal and Modula-2 — a small, strongly-typed systems language. This project implements a practical DOS dialect of Oberon-07: the core language "plus what real 16-bit systems programming needs." Programmers who are interested in experimenting with a programming language that was meant to succeed Pascal, find it at DosWorld's Trubo Oberon at GitHub.
item.source:markdown: Oberon is Niklaus Wirth's successor to Pascal and Modula-2 — a small, strongly-typed systems language. This project implements a practical DOS dialect of Oberon-07: the core language "plus what real 16-bit systems programming needs." Programmers who are interested in experimenting with a programming language that was meant to succeed Pascal, find it at DosWorld's [Trubo Oberon at GitHub](https://github.com/DosWorld/toc/).

item.title: DaynaPORT SCSI/Link packet driver for DOS
item.description: Michael Brutman created an interesting project, a basic packet driver that enables you to use the DaynaPORT SCSI/Link Ethernet device under DOS. These devices were usually used on early Macintosh computers which had SCSI controllers but not much else for expandability. Today these devices are somewhat hard to find but they are emulated by devices like BlueSCSI and ZuluSCSI. If you would like to use the DaynaPORT for networking on FreeDOS, download it at the project on GitHub.
item.source:markdown: Michael Brutman created an interesting project, a basic packet driver that enables you to use the DaynaPORT SCSI/Link Ethernet device under DOS. These devices were usually used on early Macintosh computers which had SCSI controllers but not much else for expandability. Today these devices are somewhat hard to find but they are emulated by devices like BlueSCSI and ZuluSCSI. If you would like to use the DaynaPORT for networking on FreeDOS, download it at the [project on GitHub](https://github.com/mbbrutman/daynaport-dos-packet-driver-asm).

item.title: InfoFile – a new app from Kevin Wells
item.description: If you want to find information about a file or directory from within RISC OS, the obvious thing to do in the desktop environment is to click the ‘Menu’ mouse button with the pointer over the object in a filer window, go to the submenu for it and choose ‘Info’. However, the information you get from doing that is quite limited – the ‘About this object’ window will tell you its name, its type, its size, the access rights set for it, and its date stamp. (And for a directory,…
item.source:markdown: If you want to find information about a file or directory from within RISC OS, the obvious thing to do in the desktop environment is to click the ‘Menu’ mouse button with the pointer over the object in a filer window, go to the submenu for it and choose ‘Info’. However, the information you get from doing that is quite limited – the ‘About this object’ window will tell you its name, its type, its size, the access rights set for it, and its date stamp. (And for a directory,…

item.title: Last chance for stickers and tshirts
item.description: We set up a FreeDOS store on Zazzle in 2011 so folks could buy stickers, tshirts, mugs, ties, and other items with the FreeDOS logo on them. However, maintaining the store is starting to become a hassle, so we're planning to close the Zazzle store a month from now, in August. This is your last chance to buy stickers and tshirts if you want them.
item.source:markdown: We set up a [FreeDOS store on Zazzle](https://www.zazzle.com/store/freedos) in 2011 so folks could buy stickers, tshirts, mugs, ties, and other items with the FreeDOS logo on them. However, maintaining the store is starting to become a hassle, so we're planning to close the Zazzle store a month from now, in August. This is your last chance to buy stickers and tshirts if you want them.

item.title: Multinational Apple Accounts
item.description: If you ever travel to other countries, particularly to the same ones repeatedly, register an Apple Account for each country with an easy to remember format. For example joe.uk@example.com, joe.es@example.com, joe.jp@example.com. Obviously this example only works if you own “example.com”, but you get the idea. Around the world, there are businesses whose services are available, […]
item.source:markdown: If you ever travel to other countries, particularly to the same ones repeatedly, register an Apple Account for each country with an easy to remember format. For example joe.uk@example.com, joe.es@example.com, joe.jp@example.com. Obviously this example only works if you own “example.com”, but you get the idea. Around the world, there are businesses whose services are available, \[…\]

item.description: Edition 2 of WROCC's mini magazine was sent out recently to subscribers.
item.source:markdown: Edition 2 of WROCC's mini magazine was sent out recently to subscribers.

item.title: The KIM-1 explained
item.description: The hardware and the software in the KIM-1 work closely together. The tiny program, less than 2KB in the two ROMs, together with the simple hardware, is very clever designed. The KIM-1 is a complete computer with two user interfaces and data storage with a simple namespace. It is one of the first 6502 computers, […]
item.source:markdown: The hardware and the software in the KIM-1 work closely together. The tiny program, less than 2KB in the two ROMs, together with the simple hardware, is very clever designed. The KIM-1 is a complete computer with two user interfaces and data storage with a simple namespace. It is one of the first 6502 computers, \[…\]

item.title: More than 43 Million Lines of Code in Linux Kernel 7.2
item.description: Using the cloc utility, Michael Larabel of Phoronix discovered that Linux kernel 7.2 has over 43 million lines of code.
item.source:markdown: Using the _cloc_ utility, [Michael Larabel of Phoronix](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Linux-7.2-43-Million-Lines) discovered that Linux kernel 7.2 has over 43 million lines of code.
item.author: webdevel@sparkhausmedia.com (Gwen Clark)

item.source:markdown: Mamie v0.38L (Linux), Stuff from Lidl, Cheap handheld game console.

item.title: NASM 3.02
item.description: NASM is the Netwide Assembler, an assembler for the x86 CPU architecture. NASM 3.02 was recently released with updates and fixes which should be interesting to assembly programmers. Read more in the change log. Download the new version from release build 3.02 on the NASM website. We've also mirrored the new release in the FreeDOS files archive at Ibiblio, at /devel/asm/nasm
item.source:markdown: NASM is the Netwide Assembler, an assembler for the x86 CPU architecture. NASM 3.02 was recently released with updates and fixes which should be interesting to assembly programmers. Read more in the [change log](https://www.nasm.us/docs/3.02/nasmac.html). Download the new version from [release build 3.02](https://www.nasm.us/pub/nasm/releasebuilds/3.02/) on the [NASM website](https://nasm.us/). We've also mirrored the new release in the FreeDOS files archive at Ibiblio, at [/devel/asm/nasm](https://www.ibiblio.org/pub/micro/pc-stuff/freedos/files/devel/asm/nasm/3.02/)

I’m still to this day just blown away by continental drift and things like that, stuff that most people think sounds pretty boring. — Will Wright Gamers are both extremely dedicated to and really good at preserving the history of their hobby. Seldom has a […]
item.source:markdown: This article tells part of the story of Maxis Software. item.title: KIM-1 TTY I/O, no echo, non-blocking, deaf
item.description: A page on suppressing the KIM-1 echo of TTY input, read non-blocking and make the TTY input deaf. Problems with the KIM-1 TTY character input The KIM-1 hardware is hardware echoing incoming serial characters to the output, no echo in software involved, so you cannot influence what appears on screen. Very annoying! The KIM-1 GETCH […]
item.source:markdown: A page on suppressing the KIM-1 echo of TTY input, read non-blocking and make the TTY input deaf. Problems with the KIM-1 TTY character input The KIM-1 hardware is hardware echoing incoming serial characters to the output, no echo in software involved, so you cannot influence what appears on screen. Very annoying! The KIM-1 GETCH \[…\]

item.title: FreeDOS Kernel 2046 (version 2.46)
item.description: FreeDOS Kernel is the core part of FreeDOS that provides DOS services (program loading, file and device I/O, memory management, and the INT 21h API most DOS software uses). Kernel 2046 is an incremental maintenance release with edge‑case fixes and compatibility updates. Changes in this release include: - bugfix: CD on remote Lantastic mapped drives - bugfix: print to Lantastic redirected printers - misc: port some improvements from FDPP (Stas & Andrew) - bugfix: move control-break handler off stack, fix Invisible Lan redir - bugfix: rework truename so when on mapped drive with invalid CDS properly resolves character devices (i.e. fix copy CON). Thanks to Andrew Bird and Jeremy Davis for these updates to the FreeDOS Kernel! You can find it at FreeDOS Kernel at GitHub or more directly from the FreeDOS Kernel 2046 release tag. We've also mirrored this to the FreeDOS Files Archive at Ibiblio, under files/dos/kernel.
item.source:markdown: FreeDOS Kernel is the core part of FreeDOS that provides DOS services (program loading, file and device I/O, memory management, and the INT 21h API most DOS software uses). Kernel 2046 is an incremental maintenance release with edge‑case fixes and compatibility updates. Changes in this release include: - bugfix: CD on remote Lantastic mapped drives - bugfix: print to Lantastic redirected printers - misc: port some improvements from FDPP (Stas & Andrew) - bugfix: move control-break handler off stack, fix Invisible Lan redir - bugfix: rework truename so when on mapped drive with invalid CDS properly resolves character devices (i.e. fix copy CON). Thanks to Andrew Bird and Jeremy Davis for these updates to the FreeDOS Kernel! You can find it at [FreeDOS Kernel at GitHub](https://github.com/FDOS/kernel) or more directly from the [FreeDOS Kernel 2046](https://github.com/FDOS/kernel/releases/tag/ke2046) release tag. We've also mirrored this to the FreeDOS Files Archive at Ibiblio, under [files/dos/kernel](https://www.ibiblio.org/pub/micro/pc-stuff/freedos/files/dos/kernel/2045/).

item.title: FDNPKG16 0.99.8254d
item.description: FDNPKG16 is a Network Package Manager for FreeDOS, which runs in 16-bit mode. Victoria has been making updates to the package manager, and recently released a new update. Victoria share these changes: "- cleaned a bunch of code - added basic error levels - fixed a potential bug with an error catcher and lsxflag - fixed the untested open watcom 1.9 compiling with the makefile in DOS." FDNPKG16 is open source, under the MIT license. You can download the new release from FDNPKG16 at GitHub or more directly as a zip file at fdnpkg16·zip. This is also mirrored on Victoria's personal server at fdnpkg16·zip.
item.source:markdown: FDNPKG16 is a Network Package Manager for FreeDOS, which runs in 16-bit mode. Victoria has been making updates to the package manager, and recently released a new update. Victoria share these changes: "- cleaned a bunch of code - added basic error levels - fixed a potential bug with an error catcher and lsxflag - fixed the untested open watcom 1.9 compiling with the makefile in DOS." FDNPKG16 is open source, under the MIT license. You can download the new release from [FDNPKG16 at GitHub](https://github.com/sparky4/fdnpkg16) or more directly as a zip file at [fdnpkg16·zip](https://github.com/sparky4/fdnpkg16/releases/download/0.99.8254d/fdnpkg16.zip). This is also mirrored on Victoria's personal server at [fdnpkg16·zip](https://4ch.mooo.com/freedos/repos/1.4/net/fdnpkg16.zip).

item.title: Linux Gamers May Soon See Less Mouse Lag in KDE Plasma
item.description: Gamers using KDE’s Plasma desktop have been suffering from a slight input delay in mouse movement that could lead to getting fragged.
item.source:markdown: Gamers using KDE’s Plasma desktop have been suffering from a slight input delay in mouse movement that could lead to getting fragged.
item.author: webdevel@sparkhausmedia.com (Gwen Clark)

item.title: FreeDOS is 32 years old
item.description:
It was 32 years ago today (June 29, 1994) that we announced what became the FreeDOS Project. We welcome everyone to contribute by writing code, testing, debugging, documenting, translating, advocating, .. or anything else that helps FreeDOS. Also join the email list: developers hang out on freedos-devel, general questions are on freedos-user.

You can celebrate the FreeDOS anniversary by reading one of our open books or reading any of these recent articles about FreeDOS: How FreeDOS got started, A gentle introduction to FreeDOS commands, Using variables in FreeDOS, Customize FreeDOS with FDCONFIG·SYS, Try the FED editor on FreeDOS, or this series of articles about DOS filenames: part 1, part 2, part 3. Watch Jim's presentation about FreeDOS community from DevFest'25. Buy a tshirt, sticker, or other items from the FreeDOS store on Zazzle. You can also buy a copy of Pat Villani's 1996 book about The FreeDOS Kernel.

If you'd like to see where it all started, I have shared the Alpha5 and Beta1 "distributions" on Ibiblio. These were very early and require a minimal DOS environment to start from.
item.source:markdown:
It was 32 years ago today (June 29, 1994) that we [announced](https://groups.google.com/g/comp.os.msdos.apps/c/oQmT4ETcSzU/m/O1HR8PE2u-EJ) what became the FreeDOS Project. We welcome everyone to contribute by [writing code](https://www.freedos.org/about/devel/), [testing](https://www.freedos.org/bugs/), debugging, documenting, [translating](https://shidel.github.io/fd-nls/report.html), advocating, .. or anything else that helps FreeDOS. Also join the [email list](https://www.freedos.org/forums/): developers hang out on freedos-devel, general questions are on freedos-user.
You can celebrate the FreeDOS anniversary by reading one of our [open books](https://www.freedos.org/books/) or reading any of these recent articles about FreeDOS: [How FreeDOS got started](https://www.both.org/?p=14240), [A gentle introduction to FreeDOS commands](https://www.both.org/?p=14054), [Using variables in FreeDOS](https://www.both.org/?p=14044), [Customize FreeDOS with FDCONFIG·SYS](https://www.both.org/?p=14051), [Try the FED editor on FreeDOS](https://www.both.org/?p=14056), or this series of articles about DOS filenames: [part 1](https://www.both.org/?p=14209), [part 2](https://www.both.org/?p=14211), [part 3](https://www.both.org/?p=14277). Watch Jim's presentation [about FreeDOS community](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=G3kFJg1pgrg) from DevFest'25. Buy a tshirt, sticker, or other items from the [FreeDOS store on Zazzle](https://www.zazzle.com/store/freedos). You can also buy a copy of Pat Villani's 1996 book about [The FreeDOS Kernel](https://www.routledge.com/FreeDOS-Kernel-An-MS-DOS-Emulator-for-Platform-Independence-amp-Embedded-System-Development/Villani/p/book/9780879304362).
If you'd like to see where it all started, I have shared the [Alpha5 and Beta1](https://www.ibiblio.org/pub/micro/pc-stuff/freedos/files/distributions/pre-1.0/) "distributions" on Ibiblio. These were very early and require a minimal DOS environment to start from.

item.title: WROCC July 2026 talk on Wednesday
item.description: The July 2026 WROCC talk is on Wednesday 1st July and starts at 7.45pm on Zoom. This month Steve Revill returns as speaker
item.source:markdown: The July 2026 WROCC talk is on Wednesday 1st July and starts at 7.45pm on Zoom. This month Steve Revill returns as speaker
